🇳🇱 Amsterdam Neighbourhood

Museumplein

Three world-class museums on one lawn — the Rijksmuseum, Van Gogh, and Stedelijk are all within a 5-minute walk of each other. Add Vondelpark and the best shopping street in Amsterdam and you have a full day.

In 1877, the city of Amsterdam set aside a large open field south of the Leidseplein for culture. The Rijksmuseum (1885), the Concertgebouw (1888), and what would eventually become the Van Gogh Museum and Stedelijk Museum were all built on or around this space over the following decades. The result — three world-class institutions sharing a single square kilometre of lawn — is one of the great concentrations of cultural infrastructure anywhere in Europe.

On a good day, you can walk from Rembrandt's Night Watch to Van Gogh's Sunflowers to a Mondrian in under fifteen minutes. Add the Moco Museum (Banksy and contemporary art), the Concertgebouw (free lunchtime concerts on Wednesdays), and the Vondelpark (five minutes from the museums), and you have a cultural district that rivals anything in London, Paris, or New York — at a fraction of the price.

The neighbourhood around Museumplein — Oud-Zuid — is also one of Amsterdam's most pleasant residential areas: wide leafy streets, independent cafés on Van Baerlestraat, and the PC Hooftstraat shopping street (Amsterdam's equivalent of Bond Street) for those who want to spend more than museum entry prices.

Things to Do

01

Rijksmuseum — arrive at 9am

The Rijksmuseum opens at 9am and closes at 5pm. The Night Watch is most manageable in the first hour — by 11am the Gallery of Honour is dense with tour groups. Book timed entry online. Allow 2.5 hours minimum. The museum garden (free) is worth 15 minutes of your time before or after.

02

Van Gogh Museum — book well in advance

5 minutes' walk from the Rijksmuseum, on the eastern edge of Museumplein. Online booking is mandatory — no walk-ups. The museum holds 200 paintings and 500 drawings, arranged chronologically. Friday evening sessions (6–9pm) are the quietest. Allow 1.5–2 hours.

03

Sit on the Museumplein lawn

The Museumplein lawn is free. In summer it's where Amsterdammers picnic, children play football, and tourists eat their rijsttafel takeaway with the Rijksmuseum as backdrop. In winter it hosts an outdoor skating rink. The large AMSTERDAM sign (the I AMsterdam letters have moved, but the IJ logo remains) is at the eastern end.

04

Walk to Vondelpark (5 minutes)

Vondelpark is a 5-minute walk west from the Rijksmuseum — 47 hectares of lawns, ponds, and cycling paths that serve as Amsterdam's back garden. From June to August, the open-air theatre runs free concerts and performances every weekend. No booking required.

05

PC Hooftstraat — Amsterdam's luxury shopping street

The PC Hooftstraat runs along the southern edge of Museumplein and contains Amsterdam's highest concentration of designer and luxury retail — Gucci, Hermès, Louis Vuitton, Chanel, and several Dutch designers. Even if you're not buying, the street is worth a walk for the architecture (all 19th-century townhouses) and the window displays.

06

Stedelijk Museum — same square, completely different art

The Stedelijk is directly adjacent to the Van Gogh Museum and covers modern and contemporary art from 1870 to the present — Mondrian, Kandinsky, Matisse, and Dutch Design. The controversial white "bathtub" annex (2012) is more interesting inside than the hostile exterior suggests. Rarely sells out.

Tickets & Tours

Museum

Rijksmuseum

8,000 objects, 80 rooms, the Night Watch — the greatest Dutch Golden Age collection in the world. Timed entry, book online. Arrives at 9am for the quietest experience.

Book Timed Entry
Museum

Van Gogh Museum

200 paintings, 500 drawings, the world's largest Van Gogh collection. No walk-ups accepted — online booking only. Friday evenings are the quietest time slot.

Book Van Gogh Tickets
Museum

Stedelijk Museum

Mondrian to Matisse to Dutch Design — modern and contemporary art on the same square as the Rijksmuseum and Van Gogh. Rarely sells out; good for same-day booking.

Book Stedelijk Tickets
Museum

Moco Museum

Permanent Banksy collection plus rotating contemporary shows in a 1904 villa on Museumplein. The only dedicated Banksy museum in the world. Pairs perfectly with a Rijksmuseum morning.

Book Moco Tickets

Eat & Drink

Museum-side café

Café Cobra

On the Museumplein lawn, directly opposite the Van Gogh Museum, with a terrace that looks across to the Rijksmuseum. The name comes from the COBRA art movement (Copenhagen–Brussels–Amsterdam), and the interior reflects it. A reliable option for lunch between museums — the soup and sandwiches are good value.

Breakfast / brunch

De Bakkerswinkel

A bakery and café on Roelof Hartstraat, 5 minutes from the Rijksmuseum. Arguably the best breakfast in Amsterdam — excellent bread, proper coffee, and Dutch pastries baked on the premises. Arrive before 9am on weekends to get a table.

Neighbourhood dinner

Brasserie van Baerle

The best restaurant on Van Baerlestraat — a classic French-Dutch brasserie that has been feeding Oud-Zuid residents since 1993. The steak tartare and the sole meunière are the things to order. Book ahead for evenings.

Quick lunch

Scandinavian Embassy

A Scandinavian-influenced café on Sarphatipark (15 minutes from Museumplein) that does exceptional open sandwiches (smørrebrød), good filter coffee, and the best cinnamon buns in Amsterdam. Small, often full — arrive early.

Quick Facts

🎨
Character
Cultural, residential, upscale
📍
Location
South of Leidseplein
🚶
Best way to explore
On foot between museums
⏱
Time to allow
1–2 full days (museums alone = 1 day)
🌟
Best for
Art lovers, families, shoppers
📅
Best time
Open at 9am — arrive early

Getting There

🚋
Tram 2 or 5 from Amsterdam Centraal to Hobbemastraat stop — 15 minutes. This puts you directly in front of the Rijksmuseum.
🚲
Cycling from Centraal to Museumplein takes about 15 minutes via the Singel and Leidsegracht. The bike lanes on Stadhouderskade run directly to the museum quarter.
🚶
30-minute walk from Amsterdam Centraal through the Jordaan and Vondelpark — a pleasant route that lets you see two neighbourhoods on the way to the museums.
💡
The Museumkaart (€65, valid for one month) gives unlimited entry to 400+ Dutch museums including the Rijksmuseum, Van Gogh, and Stedelijk. Worth it if you plan to visit all three.

Ready to master
the city?
